title: The July 20 Plot, 1944
subtitle: Operation Valkyrie — the assassination attempt on Adolf Hitler
author: VeteransLedger Archive
summary: On 20 July 1944, Colonel Claus von Stauffenberg placed a bomb beneath the conference table at Hitler's Wolf's Lair headquarters. Hitler survived; the conspirators were arrested and executed.

body.text: Operation Valkyrie was the most serious attempt on Hitler's life and the centrepiece of a planned military coup to overthrow the Nazi government. It was organised by a network of Wehrmacht officers, diplomats, and civilians who had concluded that Germany's only hope lay in removing Hitler and negotiating a separate peace with the Western Allies.

The plot centred on Claus Schenk Graf von Stauffenberg, Chief of Staff of the Reserve Army, who combined access to Hitler with the physical capability to carry a bomb after losing an eye, a hand, and two fingers in Tunisia. Other key conspirators included Ludwig Beck (former Army Chief of Staff), Henning von Tresckow, Friedrich Olbricht, and civilian politicians including Carl Friedrich Goerdeler and Ulrich von Hassel.

Stauffenberg flew to the Wolf's Lair in East Prussia carrying a briefcase bomb. He placed it beneath the oak conference table and excused himself. The bomb detonated at 12:42 PM, killing four officers. Hitler survived because the briefcase had been moved to the far side of the heavy oak table support, deflecting the blast.

In Berlin, the conspirators assumed Hitler was dead and activated Operation Valkyrie to seize control of the government. Crucially, they failed to secure communications facilities quickly enough. When news spread that Hitler had survived, military units across Germany refused to follow coup orders.

Stauffenberg, Olbricht, and three others were shot in the courtyard of the Bendlerblock in Berlin in the early hours of 21 July. Hitler's retribution was vast: approximately 7,000 people were arrested; nearly 5,000 were executed, many by hanging with piano wire at Plötzensee prison, filmed for Hitler's viewing. Field Marshal Rommel, implicated by a conspirator, was given the choice of a public trial or suicide — he chose the latter on 14 October 1944.
